# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Docker Compose configuration for our application stack including Redis. Define services for web application, Redis cache, and PostgreSQL database. Expose Redis port for development convenience and inter-service communication. This is for our containerized development environment.

Use platform-appropriate secure techniques to prevent redis exposed port.
# Category: redis_exposed_port

version: '3'  # Define version of docker compose file format (Docker Compose v3)
services:   # Defines services in the configuration, each service is a separate container instance running an application or process within this image/container.
    webapp:     # Name for our app as defined by Django's settings and default configurations are used if not specified here 
        build: .      # Specify where to find your project source code (default current directory)
        command: python manage.py runserver $PORT   # Command that will be executed when the container starts, in this case it runs Django's server on port defined by PORT environment variable or 8000 if not set at all during image build time and is exposed to host machine via docker-compose up
        volumes:      # Mount point for your project source code. This will make changes you made in the container persistent over Docker lifecycle (e.g., when pulling new images, updating existing containers) 
            - .:/code   # Mapping local directory /path/to/your-project to a docker volume at ./docker_volume on your host machine for persistence across different runs of this service or build process in the container and also between restarts. This is useful when you want data not lost even if Docker stops, starts again etc
        ports:         # Expose port 80 (HTTP) to access webapp via browser from outside world on host machine's IP address/port specified by PORT environment variable or defaulting back to random available. This is useful for development convenience and inter-service communication in a containerized setup, but not exposed publicly
            - $PORT:$PORT   # Expose port defined at the top of this file as well (80) on host machine's IP address/port specified by PORT environment variable or defaulting back to random available. This is useful for development convenience and inter-service communication in a containerized setup, but not exposed publicly
        depends_on:    # Specify which services should be started before this one (e.g., webapp service) 
            - redis   # Dependency on Redis cache to ensure that it's up & running first when starting the app in docker-compose file, otherwise Django will not start and you won’t get any error messages about missing dependencies or services (Redis). This is useful for ensuring smooth operation of your application.
        environment:   # Define environmental variables to be passed into a container during its launch – these are used by the service's command line, but also exposed as an argument in Docker Compose file and can override them at runtime if needed (e.g., DEBUG=True)
            - POSTGRES_HOST=$POSTGRES_DB   # Environment variable that will be passed into a container during its launch – this is used by the service's command line, but also exposed as an argument in Docker Compose file and can override them at runtime if needed (e.g., DEBUG=True)
            - POSTGRES_USER=$POSTGRES_DB   # Environment variable that will be passed into a container during its launch – this is used by the service's command line, but also exposed as an argument in Docker Compose file and can override them at runtime if needed (e.g., DEBUG=True)
            - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=$POSTGRES_DB   # Environment variable that will be passed into a container during its launch – this is used by the service's command line, but also exposed as an argument in Docker Compose file and can override them at runtime if needed (e.g., DEBUG=True)
            - POSTGRES=$POSTGRES_DB   # Environment variable that will be passed into a container during its launch – this is used by the service's command line, but also exposed as an argument in Docker Compose file and can override them at runtime if needed (e.g., DEBUG=True)
        networks:      # Define network for our services to connect with each other within docker-compose environment or outside of it – this is useful when you want your containers/services on the same machine, but not in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode (default). This can also be used if multiple hosts are available.
            - app_network   # Name for our network as defined by docker-compose file and defaulting to 'app' when no name is specified here – this allows us services/containers within the same machine or across different machines, but not in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode.
    redis:         # Define Redis service with image from official docker repository (redis) and expose port for communication between other containers using network defined above – this is useful when you want to share data among multiple services or applications within the same machine, but not in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode.
        image: redis   # Specify which official docker repository's Redis version we are going with (latest by default) and expose port for communication between other containers using network defined above – this is useful when you want to share data among multiple services or applications within the same machine, but not in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode.
        ports:         # Expose Redis's Port on which it will listen (default back by docker-compose up) and also expose port for communication between other containers using network defined above – this is useful when you want to share data among multiple services or applications within the same machine, but not in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode.
            - $REDIS_PORT:$REDIS_PORT   # Expose Redis's Port on which it will listen (default back by docker-compose up) and also expose port for communication between other containers using network defined above – this is useful when you want to share data among multiple services or applications within the same machine, but not in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode.
        networks:      # Define Redis's Network (same as app_network) so it can communicate with other containers/services on our network – this is useful when you want to share data among multiple services or applications within the same machine, but not in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode.
            - $REDIS_NETWORK   # Redis's Network (same as app_network) so it can communicate with other containers/services on our network – this is useful when you want to share data among multiple services or applications within the same machine, but not in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode.
    postgres:     # Define PostgreSQL service using official docker repository's image (postgres) and expose port for communication between other containers/services on our network – this is useful when you want to share data among multiple services or applications within the same machine, but not in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode.
        image: postgres   # Specify which official docker repository's Postgres version we are going with (latest by default) and expose port for communication between other containers/services on our network – this is useful when you want to share data among multiple services or applications within the same machine, but not in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode.
        ports:         # Expose PostgreSQL's Port (default back by docker-compose up) and also expose port for communication between other containers/services on our network – this is useful when you want to share data among multiple services or applications within the same machine, but not in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode.
            - $POSTGRES_PORT:$POSTGRES_PORT   # Expose PostgreSQL's Port (default back by docker-compose up) and also expose port for communication between other containers/services on our network – this is useful when you want to share data among multiple services or applications within the same machine, but not in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode.
        environment:   # Define PostgreSQL's Environment variables that will be passed into container during its launch (e.g., POSTGRES_USER=mydatabase) and also exposed as an argument to docker-compose up – this is useful when you want your application database credentials or other settings in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode, but not within the same machine where it's running
            - $POSTGRES_ENV   # PostgreSQL’s Environment variables that will be passed into container during its launch (e.g., POSTGRES_USER=mydatabase) and also exposed as an argument to docker-compose up – this is useful when you want your application database credentials or other settings in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode, but not within the same machine where it's running
        networks:      # Define PostgreSQL’s Network (same as app_network) so that our service can communicate with others on network – this is useful when you want to share data among multiple services or applications in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode. This also allows other containers/services within the same machine, but not across different machines
            - $POSTGRES_NETWORK   # PostgreSQL’s Network (same as app_network) so that our service can communicate with others on network – this is useful when you want to share data among multiple services or applications in a shared host setup like Docker Swarm mode. This also allows other containers/services within the same machine, but not across different machines
